authoritative_sources.title: 29 CFR Part 1904 - Recording and Reporting Occupational Injuries and Illnesses
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: The current eCFR text is the primary federal rule for injury and illness recording and reporting, making it authoritative for what incident-driven field metrics or disclosures can be claimed from official logs.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Metric eligibility and incident-report evidence review
used_for: refusal triggers when required safety logs are missing or disputed

authoritative_sources.title: 29 CFR Part 1926 - Safety and Health Regulations for Construction
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: The current eCFR text is the primary federal construction safety baseline and is authoritative for determining when changed site conditions, hazards, or stop-work conditions invalidate reporting claims.
used_for: escalation when site conditions change the answer

authoritative_sources.title: Work Permits Guide
authoritative_sources.publisher: New York City Department of Buildings
authoritative_sources.source_class: Public permitting, inspection, or field-service guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: This official public authority guide is authoritative for showing how approved plans, permits, owners, contractors, and licensed design professionals structure the legal boundary for construction work before field reporting can imply readiness.

authoritative_sources.title: Skilled Trades Project Checklists
authoritative_sources.publisher: New York City Department of Buildings
authoritative_sources.source_class: Public permitting, inspection, or field-service guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: This official public checklist is authoritative for ongoing-project, inspection, as-built, and final sign-off steps that directly affect whether field reports may state completion or closeout posture.
used_for: special inspection and final sign-off dependencies

authoritative_sources.title: Construction Quality Management for Contractors
authoritative_sources.source_class: Scheduling, subcontractor, or work-order operating standards
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: USACE construction quality management guidance is authoritative public operating guidance for contractor-vs-government reporting roles, daily quality control expectations, and accountability boundaries on construction work.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Daily field reporting ownership and review discipline

authoritative_sources.title: Contractors Quality Control Report (Daily Log of Construction)
authoritative_sources.source_class: Scheduling, subcontractor, or work-order operating standards
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: This official USACE daily QC report example is authoritative for the artifact shape and minimum evidence categories used in field reporting, including labor, equipment, weather, work activities, inspections, deficiencies, and narrative notes.
used_for: scenario realism for normal in-scope reporting

authoritative_sources.title: Commissioning Documents: Process, Contents, and Acceptance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: WBDG is a recognized federal and industry public building guidance platform. This reference is authoritative for issues logs, field reports, testing records, systems manuals, and final commissioning reports used in closeout and turnover reporting packages.

authoritative_sources.title: Project Management Guide: Design/Build-Bridging Project Delivery Methods
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: GSA's public construction project management guide is authoritative for substantial completion, punch-list, final cleaning, and turnover milestones that constrain closeout reporting claims on public construction projects.

authoritative_sources.title: What is Construction Management?
authoritative_sources.source_class: Scheduling, subcontractor, or work-order operating standards
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: CMAA is a recognized professional authority for construction management practice, and this public role definition is authoritative for keeping time, cost, quality, and coordination reporting distinct from permitting, design, or licensed trade authority.
used_for: refusal triggers for action ownership requests

workflow_stages.name: Source collection and qualification
workflow_stages.description: Collect work-order, permit, inspection, daily-report, schedule, safety, subcontractor, and closeout records and determine whether the evidence set is current and sufficient for reporting.
source_classes: Public permitting, inspection, or field-service guidance
Scheduling, subcontractor, or work-order operating standards

workflow_stages.name: Metric definition and record normalization
workflow_stages.description: Resolve metric logic, date windows, denominator rules, and record-precedence handling before analytics or narrative reporting claims are assembled.
source_classes: Scheduling, subcontractor, or work-order operating standards

workflow_stages.name: Narrative synthesis and disclosure-risk review
workflow_stages.description: Assemble field status, progress, caveats, and report language while checking that no unsupported compliance, completion, safety, or causal claims are introduced.
source_classes: Scheduling, subcontractor, or work-order operating standards

workflow_stages.name: Submission or reporting package assembly
workflow_stages.description: Package metric definitions, source ties, open blockers, and evidence dependencies for internal or external reporting cadences without implying final authority.
source_classes: Public permitting, inspection, or field-service guidance

workflow_stages.name: Exception and adjacent-authority escalation
workflow_stages.description: Escalate stale, conflicting, incomplete, safety-altering, permit-sensitive, inspection-sensitive, or licensed-authority-sensitive cases instead of synthesizing unsupported certainty.
source_classes: Public permitting, inspection, or field-service guidance
Scheduling, subcontractor, or work-order operating standards

artifacts: permits, approved plans, and permit status records
inspection requests, inspection reports, deficiency notices, and special inspection letters
daily logs and contractor quality control reports
schedule updates, percent-complete records, delay logs, and look-aheads
subcontractor status updates and coordination records
safety observations, incident or near-miss records, and stop-work notices
field photos, installation evidence, punch lists, as-builts, and closeout packages

allowed: Classify field reporting requests as in-scope, out-of-scope, or blocked on missing prerequisites.
Define or validate reporting metrics, source lineage, and caveat language from construction and field-service records.
Prepare reporting packages, reconciliation notes, and disclosure-risk escalations without overstating permit, code, inspection, or licensed-trade authority.
escalate: Required tenant facts, record locations, metric definitions, or source precedence rules are missing and materially change the output.
Permit status, inspection posture, safety incidents, stop-work conditions, or closeout blockers materially change the reportable answer.
Public guidance, jurisdiction rules, or tenant policy conflict on metric eligibility, readiness, completion, or disclosure language.
Requests cross into project controls ownership, superintendent action ownership, legal interpretation, design authority, or licensed trade signoff.
refuse: Requests to imply permit approval, code interpretation, licensed trade signoff, engineer-of-record judgment, or final inspection acceptance.
Requests to publish unsupported causal, policy, compliance, or final-completion claims not supported by the evidence set.
Requests to approve change orders, direct subcontractor work, negotiate with inspectors, or override safety, permit, inspection, or closeout blockers.

domain_failure_modes: Publishing a field-progress or completion metric even though percent-complete logic is disputed across schedule, QC, and inspection records.
Treating stale subcontractor, inspection, or permit records as good enough for a current report and overstating readiness or completion.
Confusing substantial completion or work installed with final acceptance, final inspection sign-off, or customer turnover completion.
Turning field reporting into project-controls, superintendent, legal, or licensed-trade authority instead of staying inside evidence-backed reporting support.
Omitting safety or stop-work changes that materially alter the reportable posture for the same reporting period.
Producing generic status reporting that ignores work-order, permit, inspection, subcontractor, and closeout artifact requirements specific to construction and field services.

scenario_family_coverage.scenario_family: Request that implies licensed trade or code-interpretation authority
Metric definition is ambiguous or disputed
Source data is incomplete, stale, or conflicting
Stakeholder asks for a claim or disclosure not supported by the data
Submission inputs are incomplete or conflicting
A requested claim is not fully supported by the evidence set

unresolved_ambiguities.resolution_note: The public baseline defines reporting discipline, but each tenant must nominate whether installed, earned, inspected, or accepted quantities control external reporting.

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: Jurisdiction-specific permit and final sign-off sequence
unresolved_ambiguities.resolution_note: Public permit and inspection references illustrate the workflow, but tenants must supply the governing jurisdiction and the exact authority having jurisdiction sequence before case-specific completion claims are made.

notes: The source set covers workflow, evidence, terminology, and failure handling rather than only one dimension.
No blocking unresolved ambiguity remains because tenant-specific metric and jurisdiction choices are represented explicitly as required runtime inputs rather than guessed.
